stories.title: Opportunity Zone 2.0: Beefing Up an Investment Tool
stories.excerpt: Congress created the Opportunity Zone program as part of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017. The initial Qualified Opportunity Zone (QOZ) program offered tax incentives to investors who invested capital gains from the sale of assets into a Qualified Opportunity Fund (QOF). The fund would then put that money toward improving designated economically distressed areas. Opportunity Zone 1.0 attracted tens of billions of dollars in private investment since its creation, providing capital to commercial real estate projects in designated communities. Nearly a decade later, lawmakers decided to refine the program, introducing a permanent Qualified Opportunity Zone framework as part of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. Commercial real estate attorneys told Connect CRE that the changes could make QOFs more attractive to investors by extending tax benefits, providing more certainty and offering incentives for rural development. “One of the biggest issues we ran into with QOZ 1.0 was that the initial framework that was passed in 2017 did not include a lot of the technical details needed to really take advantage of the program,” said Laura Cable, a partner with Cox, Castle & Nicholson. “With version 2.0, we don’t have that same hurdle so that we may see an influx of potential investors looking for QOFs in January.” More Clarity Laura Cable Under the original law, Qualified Opportunity Zone benefits are scheduled to expire on Dec. 31, 2028. The new legislation eliminated the deadlines, making the program permanent. State governors are required to redesignate QOZs every ten years on a rolling basis, beginning July 1, 2026. “The general framework remains the same,” said Troutman Pepper Locke partner Thomas Phelan. “The program continues to encourage investment in economically disadvantaged communities through tax incentives.” However, the new framework narrows eligibility by redefining median income requirements and eliminating
bullets: Congress passed Opportunity Zone 2.0 as part of One Big Beautiful Bill Act, permanently extending QOZ tax incentives.
New framework adds technical clarity missing from 2017 version, making QOF structures more investor-friendly and certain.
Version 2.0 introduces rural development incentives, broadening capital deployment beyond distressed urban cores.
stories.summary: Opportunity Zone 2.0 makes the original tax-incentive program permanent and adds technical precision, extending capital gains tax deferral and step-up benefits for investors deploying capital through Qualified Opportunity Funds into designated economically distressed areas. The revised framework removes ambiguities from QOZ 1.0 that limited investor participation and adds explicit incentives for rural projects. Legal experts expect this will unlock new waves of private capital into secondary and tertiary markets.
stories.impact: Multifamily developers in Opportunity Zone-designated secondary and tertiary markets gain access to a larger, more predictable capital pool. Extended certainty on tax benefits reduces investor friction and widens the geographic range of economically viable development—especially beneficial for value-add and stabilization plays outside primary metros.

stories.title: Is Space the Next Frontier for Data Centers?
stories.excerpt: The explosive demand for artificial intelligence and cloud computing has led to an unprecedented data center construction boom. But as developers search for suitable land and utilities, they’re increasingly running into the same obstacles: limited power, water constraints, rising development costs and community opposition. Andrew Batson Those challenges have prompted the consideration of possible alternatives once considered science fiction: building, operating and maintaining data centers in space. A recent JLL report suggested that orbital data centers could one day partner with their terrestrial counterparts by handling energy-intensive workloads such as AI training, scientific simulations and satellite data processing. While the concept is years away from reality, there has been serious consideration as to whether moving some computing capacity off the planet could help address the growing limitations of building on it. “These are not problems that incremental efficiency gains will solve,” Andrew Batson, JLL’s global head of data center research, told Connect CRE. “Earth is becoming harder to build on, pushing operators to consider alternatives.” Why Space? The concept is less far-fetched than it might sound. The International Space Station already operates computing equipment, including central processing units and graphics processing units, although on a much smaller scale than what’s now being proposed. “The ambition and physical scale being proposed has shifted, with a single data center being larger than the entire ISS,” Batson said. Major technology companies are already exploring orbital computing. SpaceX has filed a request with the FCC seeking approval for a constellation of data center satellites. Amazon’s AWS Ground Station is developing infrastructure that would connect Earth-based and orbital workloads, while Microsoft’s Azure Orbital and Google’s Project Suncatcher are explorin
bullets: AI and cloud computing boom driving unprecedented data center demand; terrestrial constraints now binding.
Developers face power, water, land limits and rising costs, prompting exploration of orbital alternatives.
Concept remains years away but signals fundamental shift in data center land scarcity across all major metros.
stories.summary: Explosive demand for AI and cloud infrastructure has overwhelmed terrestrial data center development, creating severe bottlenecks in power, water, and available land. JLL reports developers are seriously exploring orbital data centers as an alternative to handle energy-intensive workloads. While deployment is years off, the constraints underscore that earth-based capacity expansion faces hard limits.
stories.impact: As orbital data centers remain theoretical, terrestrial data center scarcity will intensify competition for power-rich, water-accessible sites in secondary metros (Phoenix, Austin, Texas corridor). This tightens land availability and utility costs, which indirectly reduces feasible locations for mixed-use and multifamily projects needing co-development with data infrastructure. Developers should expect rising land and utility costs in data-center-adjacent submarkets.

stories.title: Digging into the Labor Outlook and What it Means for CRE
stories.excerpt: Headlines announcing the Bureau of Labor Statistics’ employment numbers tend to forget one thing: Those numbers are preliminary, only. For example, April and May saw a large hiring surge, which came to a crashing halt in June, with preliminary numbers showing the addition of 57,000 jobs. Meanwhile, the much-vaunted April and May figures were revised downward by a combined 74,000 jobs. Breaking this down, the average monthly job gains over the past six months stood at 92,000. “Recent reporting volatility, along with large downward benchmark revisions in prior years, suggests first-cut payroll estimates should be treated with caution,” a recently released Marcus & Millichap brief said. At the same time, unevenness in the labor market could have an impact on selective leasing activity, “with tenants prioritizing renewals, efficient layouts and incremental expansions,” the brief said. Other highlights include the following: Softer hiring and a thinner labor pool could increase the role of productivity in sustaining output. This could mean office demand is more focused on renewals and higher-quality space. Hiring trends were weakest among industries relying on discretionary spending. This includes Leisure & Hospitality and Retail. The brief said this could favor necessity-based retail leasing, while large-ticket items could face slower growth. Stronger pay gains for those who switch jobs might support in-migration, household formation and apartment demand, especially near employment centers. The post Digging into the Labor Outlook and What it Means for CRE appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: June 2026 job growth only 57K; April-May figures revised down 74K combined—labor volatility persists.
Six-month average job gains 92K/month, well below pre-pandemic trend; hiring weakness in Leisure & Hospitality, Retail.
Softer demand signals office leasing focused on renewals, quality space, and incremental expansions over new floor-plate demand.
stories.summary: U.S. job growth remains volatile and significantly below historical trend, with June 2026 adding only 57,000 jobs and prior months revised sharply downward. Hiring weakness concentrates in discretionary-spending sectors (Leisure, Hospitality, Retail), while the labor pool is thinning. Employers are shifting to productivity and retention over expansion, favoring renewals and efficient layouts.
stories.impact: Softening labor markets reduce corporate tenant demand for large new office contraction, directly dampening office market absorption—but also signaling downstream pressure on multifamily demand if white-collar employment falters in key metros. Developers should expect slower renter household formation in markets heavily weighted to discretionary-spending employment (hospitality hubs, retail corridors), favoring supply constraint over new delivery in secondary markets.

stories.title: The Sunbelt and Multifamily: Oversupply Isn’t the Whole Story
stories.excerpt: Casey MacMaster For the past two years, one word has dominated conversations about the Sunbelt apartment market: oversupply. The numbers behind the narrative are real. A wave of multifamily projects that broke ground during the pandemic construction boom came online between 2023 and 2025, flooding many fast-growing markets throughout the Southwest and Southeast with new inventory. Rent growth slowed, concessions increased, and occupancy rates declined, fueling headlines that painted the entire region as overbuilt. But industry experts told Connect CRE that the reality is more nuanced. While the metrics are accurate, treating the Sunbelt as a single apartment market overlooks the differences in local economies, demand, demographics and market fundamentals that determine whether new supply is a problem or a temporary growing pain. “I would not say the supply numbers themselves are exaggerated,” said Casey MacMaster, Bonaventure’s senior vice president, investment and portfolio manager. “The numbers are what they are. The consideration is how the narrative is framed.” More Than a Market One of the largest problems with the oversupply narrative is classifying the Sunbelt as a single entity. It’s not. It’s a broad geographic label covering roughly 15 to 18 states stretching from Southern California across the Southwest and Gulf Coast to the Southeast. Those states have different economies, regulatory environments and business climates. Texas, Florida, Georgia and Tennessee consistently rank among CNBC’s top states for business, while Mississippi and Louisiana rank near the bottom. California’s economy is driven by technology, entertainment and aerospace, while South Carolina continues to attract advanced manufacturing and industrial investment. Ashley Stefan These different jobs economies attract different populations. And those populations drive apartment demand. “The biggest misconception is that the Sunbelt is a single,
bullets: Sunbelt pandemic-era delivery (2023–2025) flooded markets with inventory; rents slowed, concessions rose, occupancy fell.
Oversupply narrative treats Sunbelt as monolithic; reality: local economics, demographics, and demand drivers vary sharply.
Markets differentiated by employment diversity, population growth, and in-migration patterns; supply imbalance temporary in supply-constrained submarkets.
stories.summary: The Sunbelt absorbed a massive wave of pandemic-era multifamily starts between 2023 and 2025, creating perceived oversupply across the region. However, industry sources push back on treating the Sunbelt as a single market—local fundamentals (job growth, migration, affordability) vary significantly. Some submarkets remain supply-constrained despite regional headlines of overbuilding.
stories.impact: Developers should isolate submarket fundamentals rather than follow Sunbelt-wide supply narratives. Markets with strong in-migration (Austin, Nashville, parts of North Carolina, Florida metros) likely remain supply-constrained despite regional softness; acquisition and underwriting should reflect local absorption and household formation, not regional rhetoric. Trophy assets and newer construction in high-demand micro-markets will outperform.

stories.title: Rivani Sells Wynwood Jungle Retail Building in Miami for $26M
stories.excerpt: Prominent real estate investor Robert Rivani has sold the Wynwood Jungle retail building in Miami for $25.7 million, property records show. Tabani Group purchased the 39,000-square-foot property between 75 and 43 Northwest 23rd Street, west of North Miami Avenue. The deal is equal to almost $660 per square foot. Tenants on the ground floor include Häagen-Dazs and doughnut purveyor The Salty. Fabel, an upscale restaurant on the rooftop, recently shuttered. About half of the leasable space remains available, according to a listing. Frost Bank supplied a $15.7 million mortgage to the Dallas-based buyer, an investor specialized in retail and multifamily asset classes. Rivani purchased the property for $13.3 million in 2021. Under his ownership, Wynwood Jungle has been embroiled in two lawsuits. One involved a steakhouse from Goat Hospitality owner Derek Gonzalez that ultimately did not open. Rivani sued this year, alleging he is owed $9.1 million in back rent, Business Journals reported. Restaurateur Brian Swanson sued, claiming the landlord provided only 6,158 square feet, instead of 7,751 square feet stipulated in the lease, The Real Deal reported. Rivani contended that the suit was a ploy to break the lease. The transaction represents the latest sale for Rivani, who has been offloading retail properties typically after signing leases with high-end restaurants. Last year, the investor sold the space housing Bad Bunny’s Gekko in Brickell, and Amara at Paraiso in Edgewater and Catch in Miami Beach for $86 million. Representatives for Tabani Group and Rivani did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Wynwood Jungle retail building sold for $25.7M ($660/sf) by Rivani to Tabani Group; Frost Bank financed $15.7M.
Property 39K sf with 50% vacancy; ground floor anchors Häagen-Dazs, Salty doughnuts; Fabel restaurant shuttered.
Asset carried litigation burden from failed tenant deals and back-rent disputes; distressed liquidity event.
stories.summary: Investor Robert Rivani sold the Wynwood Jungle retail property in Miami to Tabani Group for $25.7M after prolonged operational and legal friction. The 39,000-sf asset trades at $660/sf with roughly 50% occupancy, weighed down by failed restaurant openings and pending litigation over back rent. Frost Bank provided $15.7M financing to the Dallas-based buyer.
stories.impact: Retail-mixed-use volatility in Miami's Wynwood—once a booming submarket—signals softening restaurant/hospitality demand and tenant credit stress in experiential retail. Developers with land or redevelopment exposure in Wynwood should expect lower lease rates, longer lease-up, and higher TI requirements for new ground-floor retail, particularly for F&B uses. Adds pressure to retail NOI models in secondary Miami infill sites.

stories.title: Ascendant Capital Partners Acquires Virginia Hotel Portfolio
stories.excerpt: Ascendant Capital Partners has acquired an eight-hotel portfolio from Coastal Hospitality Associates, LLC. The Portfolio comprises 965 predominantly beachfront rooms across six hotels in Virginia Beach and two hotels in the Outer Banks. Ascendant will seek to implement targeted capital and operational enhancements across the portfolio, while Schulte Hospitality Group will assume operations. The portfolio consists of SpringHill Suites by Marriott Virginia Beach Oceanfront, Residence Inn by Marriott Virginia Beach Town Center, Residence Inn by Marriott Virginia Beach Oceanfront, Fairfield by Marriott Inn & Suites Virginia Beach Oceanfront, Holiday Inn VA Beach-Oceanside, Holiday Inn Express & Suites VA Beach Oceanfront, Holiday Inn Express Nags Head Oceanfront, and Holiday Inn Express Kitty Hawk – Outer Banks. CBRE Hotels served as the sell-side advisor to Coastal Hospitality, and CBRE Debt & Structured Finance served as the financing advisor to Ascendant. “This transaction reflects the continued execution of our differentiated hospitality strategy, which is focused on high-quality assets in dynamic, supply-constrained markets with resilient demand drivers,” said Alex Halpern, Co-Founder and Chief Investment Officer of Ascendant. The post Ascendant Capital Partners Acquires Virginia Hotel Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Ascendant Capital Partners acquired 8-hotel portfolio (965 rooms) from Coastal Hospitality: Virginia Beach and Outer Banks.
Portfolio brands: SpringHill Suites, Residence Inn, Fairfield, Holiday Inn express and standard; Schulte Hospitality manages.
CBRE Hotels and CBRE Debt & Structured Finance advised; deal signals continued capital flow into beachfront, supply-constrained hospitality.
stories.summary: Ascendant Capital Partners acquired an eight-hotel portfolio of 965 mostly beachfront rooms across Virginia Beach and Outer Banks from Coastal Hospitality. Portfolio consists of mid-market brands (Marriott franchises, Holiday Inn) and will be operationally enhanced by Schulte Hospitality. Deal highlights institutional appetite for high-quality assets in supply-constrained, resilient coastal markets.
stories.impact: Virginia Beach and Outer Banks beachfront continue attracting institutional capital on supply scarcity and tourism resilience. For multifamily developers: limited beachfront land availability in these markets will keep land costs elevated and reduce mixed-use opportunities. Hospitality anchors in these submarkets remain strong demand drivers for complementary residential, but land scarcity limits build opportunity to higher-density projects only.

stories.title: Nvidia Signs Downtown D.C. Office Lease as Trophy Buildings Outperform
stories.excerpt: The most valuable company in the country is expanding its presence in the nation’s capital. NVIDIA signed an office lease in Washington, D.C., to become the latest technology company to take space in the District while demand concentrates in the market’s premier buildings. The artificial intelligence chipmaker signed a 27,600-square-foot sublease at Douglas Development’s Woodies Building at 1025 F Street NW, according to Cushman & Wakefield’s second-quarter market report. The space is being subleased from Morning Consult, which leased the building’s entire 50,000-square-foot eighth floor in 2020, according to Bisnow. Nvidia also maintains an office in Herndon, Va. The new Nvidia lease adds another high-profile tenant to the Woodies Building, where Netflix is also opening a 24,000-square-foot office and entertainment space. The building has simultaneously been backfilling space previously occupied by the FBI, whose roughly 200,000-square-foot lease expired last year. Nvidia also reinforces a trend of tech firms expanding in Downtown Washington, which has seen CoreWeave, SpaceX and potentially Google taking new deals. Washington’s office recovery remains uneven while its premier buildings have separated themselves from the rest of the market. Cushman & Wakefield reported trophy office vacancy finished the quarter at just 13.4 percent, while overall vacancy climbed to 23.3 percent in the second quarter. Gross leasing totaled 1.6 million square feet during the quarter, and 2.9 million square feet during the first half, down roughly 12 percent from the same period last year. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Nvidia signed 27.6K sf sublease at Woodies Building (1025 F Street NW) in Downtown D.C.; subleased from Morning Consult.
Netflix also leasing 24K sf at Woodies; trophy building backfilling FBI's 200K sf departed lease.
Tech expansion in Downtown D.C. concentrates in premier buildings; trophy assets outperforming secondary office stock.
stories.summary: Nvidia, the world's most valuable company, expanded its D.C. footprint by signing a 27,600-sf sublease at the Woodies Building, a trophy asset in Downtown D.C. Netflix is also entering the building with 24,000 sf. The Woodies is simultaneously absorbing backfill from the FBI's departure, with tech tenants leading occupancy recovery. Downtown D.C.'s office market is concentrating tenant demand in premier buildings.
stories.impact: Trophy office assets in major metros (here D.C.) are decoupling from secondary stock; tech and AI-driven employers clustering in A+ buildings signals bifurcation. For multifamily developers in D.C.: strong tech in-migration (Nvidia, Amazon, continued growth) supports residential demand in walkable, transit-proximate neighborhoods near Downtown and Herndon tech corridors. However, office oversupply in secondary buildings will depress landlord capital and limit mixed-use development ROI outside trophy blocks.

stories.title: Bobby Zar Snaps Up 827 Broadway for $19M Via Short Sale
stories.excerpt: There’s a changing of the guard in Greenwich Village. Bobby Zar’s ZG Capital Partners has snapped up the interconnected buildings at 827-831 Broadway, plus 47 East 12th Street, for $18.5 million via a short sale, sources told Commercial Observer. Newmark’s Brett Siegel, Maurice Suede and Adam Spies negotiated the lender-directed sale on behalf of the seller, while ZG Capital’s Zar and James Tamborlane led the transaction in-house. The buildings, which sit at the eastern edge of Greenwich Village just south of Union Square, sold for less than a third of the price they traded for just over a decade ago, representing a steep haircut. Quality Capital and Caerus purchased the four-story buildings in 2015 for $60 million, with plans to bring a 300-foot, 14-story office and retail building to the address, The Real Deal reported at the time. The plan was to demolish the existing cast-iron structures and build 11 stories of office floors atop three stories of retail. In May 2017, the borrowers recapitalized their investment, taking a $59 million loan from LoanCore Capital. However, in October 2017, following a campaign led by Village Preservation that fought for landmark designation, the New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission put a stop to the developers’ plans, granting landmark status to 827-831 Broadway and preventing the buildings’ demolition due to their significant cultural and historical significance. (The 12th Street building was not designated.) Indeed, the buildings have quite the rich history. In the 19th century, the Broadway buildings were home to the Wilson Sewing Machine Company as well as A.A. Vantine, the first importer of Japanese arts into the U.S. In the 1950s and 1960s, the top floors of the Griffith Thomas-designed, Italianate structures served as studios for prominent artists such as abstract expressionist artist Willem de Kooning, who painted his famous “Pastorale” at 831 Broadway in 1963. It wasn’t immediately clear what the plans are for th
bullets: Bobby Zar's ZG Capital purchased 827-831 Broadway + 47 East 12th Street via short sale for $18.5M.
Building sold for ~33% of 2015 price ($60M); prior development plan (300-ft office tower) abandoned.
Greenwich Village/Union Square site represents distressed redevelopment asset; office development economics no longer viable at pre-pandemic underwriting.
stories.summary: ZG Capital Partners acquired the interconnected Greenwich Village buildings at 827-831 Broadway and 47 East 12th Street for $18.5M via short sale—roughly one-third the $60M purchase price from 2015. The prior plan for a 14-story office tower was abandoned. The steep haircut reflects collapsed office development returns in Manhattan and prior owner distress.
stories.impact: Manhattan office development economics have inverted dramatically since 2015; distressed sales signal that mid-market office conversions (to residential or hospitality) are more viable than traditional office development. For multifamily developers in NYC: distressed office sites near Union Square and high-amenity neighborhoods may present conversion opportunities at favorable basis, though entitlements and construction costs remain elevated. Signals prolonged office distress in secondary-plus markets.

stories.title: NBCUniversal Renews 244K-SF Offices at 1221 Avenue of the Americas
stories.excerpt: NBCUniversal is keeping its massive office near Rockefeller Center. The mass media and entertainment conglomerate has renewed its 244,185-square-foot lease at Rockefeller Group’s 1221 Avenue of the Americas, according to a second-quarter office report from CBRE. NBC first signed a lease for its space at the 51-story Midtown office building in 2012 as part of a $190 million investment into its New York City operations, according to an announcement at the time. That investment also included a series of upgrades and renovations to NBC’s headquarters across the street at 30 Rockefeller Plaza. The length of the new renewal and the asking rent were unclear, but CBRE’s report found office asking rents in Midtown averaged $86.18 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026. It’s also unclear who brokered the deal. Rockefeller Group declined to comment, while spokespeople for CBRE, NBC and Cushman & Wakefield — which has represented Rockefeller Group in past deals at 1221 Avenue of the Americas — did not immediately respond to requests for comment. NBC last signed a renewal deal at the Midtown property in 2021, when it extended a lease for a secondary office at the building, bringing its overall footprint up to 339,933 square feet. It’s unclear whether the new renewal is only for part of NBC’s office space at the property, or whether NBC reduced its total office footprint there. Nevertheless, news of NBC’s new deal comes during a busy period for office renewals in Manhattan. And those renewals happen to be some of the largest leases signed during the second quarter, according to CBRE’s report. Down at One Liberty Plaza in the Financial District, AON, a global professional services firm that provides risk, retirement and health solutions, renewed 201,931 square feet, the report found. Meanwhile, over in Hudson Square, WNYC Radio, New York City’s flagship public radio station, signed a renewal for 87,283 square feet at 160 Varick Street, according to CBRE. Overall, Ma
bullets: NBCUniversal renewed 244.2K sf lease at 1221 Avenue of Americas (Midtown, Rockefeller Group property).
Lease originally signed 2012 as part of $190M NBC investment into NYC operations; renewal length/rate not disclosed.
Midtown office asking rent averages $86.18/sf Q2 2026; major anchor renewal signals trophy building resilience.
stories.summary: NBCUniversal renewed its 244,185-sf lease at 1221 Avenue of the Americas, a 51-story Midtown office tower owned by Rockefeller Group. The renewal follows NBC's original 2012 lease and $190M investment into NYC operations. Renewal terms (length and rent) were not disclosed, but Midtown average asking rents reached $86.18/sf in Q2 2026.
stories.impact: Large media and tech anchor renewals in trophy Midtown buildings indicate selective, high-quality office demand remains resilient, but are concentrated in A+ properties with premium positioning. Secondary Midtown and outer-borough office continues to struggle. For multifamily: strong anchor tenant renewals in premium office corridors underscore bifurcation and support residential demand near Class-A office in Manhattan and key secondary metros; tertiary office corridors remain headwind to mixed-use development returns.

stories.title: Heart to Heart Homecare Takes 13K SF for Office in South Bronx
stories.excerpt: Heart to Heart Homecare, a home health and personal care provider, is bringing its services to the South Bronx. The home care services agency, which provides nonmedical assistance to seniors and individuals with disabilities, has signed a 10-year, 12,500-square-foot lease for a new office at 369 148th Street, Commercial Observer has learned. Asking rent for the space was $38 per square foot, according to a source close to the deal. Nick Zweig from Locations Commercial Real Estate was the sole broker on the deal. Zweig and Heart to Heart Homecare did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s requests for comment. Heart to Heart Homecare provides services in all five boroughs, and offers health care and social services to people who are elderly, disabled and injured. The company also has an office in East Orange, N.J. The four-story South Bronx property, which is owned by Marc Fishman of Edith Court LLC, was built in 1931 and spans 55,500 square feet, according to Loopnet.com. The Bronx Legal Services, Nexus Medical Training Center and the Bronx Peer Advocacy Center are also tenants at 369 148th Street. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Heart to Heart Homecare signed 10-year, 12.5K sf lease in South Bronx (369 148th Street) at $38/sf.
Home health and eldercare services expanding across NYC boroughs; South Bronx emerging secondary office market.
Four-story building (55.5K sf) houses Bronx Legal Services, Nexus Medical Training Center, other mission-aligned tenants.
stories.summary: Heart to Heart Homecare, a nonmedical home health provider, signed a 10-year, 12,500-sf lease in the South Bronx at $38/sf. The company serves seniors and disabled individuals across all five NYC boroughs. The South Bronx building at 369 148th Street is becoming a hub for health services and mission-driven tenants.
stories.impact: Healthcare and eldercare services expanding in South Bronx signals demographic aging and institutional landlord interest in secondary borough office. For multifamily developers: growing elderly and disabled populations in outer boroughs create demand for senior housing and accessible multifamily units; proximity to health service nodes (like 369 148th Street) is emerging submarket value driver. Low office rents ($38/sf) reflect limited competition, creating cost-efficient co-location opportunities for mixed-use health/residential projects.

stories.title: Vernon Hills clears 1.2M sf industrial redevelopment on former American Hotel Register site
stories.excerpt: Redevelopment of an ex-American Hotel Register property northwest of Chicago was approved,  greenlighting an additional 1 million-square-foot industrial park in the region.  The Vernon Hills site spans 70 acres and is set for 1.2 million square feet spread across three speculative buildings, one existing warehouse and a site for hand2mind, a classroom supplies and teacher curriculum company. An entity listed as JCA Hayes LLC has been trying to get this project in motion since late 2024, according to the Chicago Daily Herald. The planned site sits at 100-230 South Milwaukee Avenue.  At one point, it appeared that the land was […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Vernon Hills (northwest Chicago) approved 1.2M sf industrial redevelopment on 70-acre American Hotel Register site.
Project includes three speculative warehouses, one existing warehouse, and hand2mind (classroom supplies) manufacturing facility.
Amazon 'Project Zeus' proposal (3M sf) withdrawn 2022; current plan (JCA Hayes LLC) approved with sound-wall conditions and traffic mitigation.
stories.summary: Vernon Hills, Illinois approved a 1.2M-sf industrial redevelopment on a 70-acre former American Hotel Register campus. The project includes three new speculative buildings, one retained warehouse, and a hand2mind classroom-supplies manufacturing facility. The prior Amazon-linked 'Project Zeus' proposal (3M sf) was withdrawn; the revised plan was approved with conditional requirements for sound walls and traffic mitigation.
stories.impact: Chicago-area industrial supply is expanding in secondary suburbs (Vernon Hills) with speculative warehouse production; this signals confidence in e-commerce and logistics demand despite macro softness. For multifamily developers in Chicago suburbs: large industrial corridors (Vernon Hills, adjacent markets) create population anchor and shift worker housing demand toward industrial parks. Developers should monitor speculative absorption rates in such industrial parks to gauge whether wages/employment will sustain near-term residential construction.

stories.title: Caterpillar digs into new $34M HQ expansion in Las Colinas
stories.excerpt: Caterpillar, a worldwide construction and engineering manufacturer that calls suburban Dallas home, is planning an expansion of its headquarters around three miles down the road from its current one at the Towers at Williams Square. The company is expected to spend $36 million to renovate portions of an office property at 901 West Walnut Hill Lane in Irving. Initial phases of the project are slated to cover 107,000 square feet and are expected to be completed around August 2027, according to the Dallas Morning News. The total square footage of the Las Colinas offices is pegged at over 400,000 square […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Caterpillar investing $34M to expand HQ in Las Colinas (Irving, TX), expanding 107K sf initially by August 2027.
Company consolidating operations within DFW; renovating former Zales/Vari campus (901 West Walnut Hill Lane).
Total Las Colinas footprint over 400K sf; company relocated to DFW from Illinois in 2022; workforce expansion planned.
stories.summary: Caterpillar is investing $34M to expand its headquarters at a former Zales/Vari campus in Irving's Las Colinas district, near its current Towers at Williams Square office. Initial phase covers 107,000 sf, with total expected footprint exceeding 400,000 sf by end of 2027. The expansion reflects the company's 2022 relocation from Deerfield, Illinois and plans to grow its North Texas workforce.
stories.impact: Caterpillar's major HQ expansion and workforce growth in DFW anchors white-collar job creation in suburban Irving/Las Colinas. For multifamily developers: this signals sustained corporate office demand and employment growth in North Texas suburbs, underpinning residential demand in Irving, Coppell, and surrounding areas. Proximity to major employer campuses (CAT, tech centers, financial services) makes nearby multifamily sites high-probability acquisitions for build-to-hold or value-add strategies.

stories.title: Pendry Nashville enlists Douglas Elliman, broker to woo New York condo buyers
stories.excerpt: A development advisory firm is wagering that a formula crafted in New York combining an intimate live music concert series with philanthropy can draw interest to a high-end Nashville condo project.  Redeavor, the two-year-old firm serving as the exclusive sales and marketing team for the Pendry Residences Nashville, has tapped Douglas Elliman and one of its longtime brokers, Greg Williamson, as New York City marketing partners.  Developed by SomeraRoad, the 30-story, $364 million tower is slated to include 146 condo units on the upper floors, as well as a 180-key hotel. Redeavor’s Maranda Blanton said they anticipate construction will be […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Pendry Residences Nashville (30-story, $364M, 146 condos + 180-key hotel) enlists Douglas Elliman and NYC broker Greg Williamson.
Project closed $26M in pre-sales in Q1 2026 (penthouse ~$7M); unit pricing starts ~$1.3M; completion end of 2027.
SomeraRoad developing wider Gulch (Paseo South Gulch) with retail, office, and 278-apartment complex (opened 2024).
stories.summary: SomeraRoad's Pendry Residences Nashville, a $364M mixed-use tower with 146 condos and 180-key hotel, hired Douglas Elliman and NYC broker Greg Williamson to market units to East Coast buyers. The project has closed $26M in pre-sale contracts; penthouses sold for ~$7M; base pricing starts ~$1.3M. Completion expected late 2027 with hotel opening early 2028. Part of broader Gulch revitalization (Paseo South Gulch).
stories.impact: High-end condo pre-sales in Nashville's Gulch signal strong out-of-state capital inflow and lifestyle migration to Nashville. For multifamily developers: luxury residential demand from NYC-based and coastal buyers is sustaining Nashville's rent and price growth despite Sunbelt oversupply headlines. Market differentiation (trophy mixed-use, hospitality anchor, entertainment district) matters; generic multifamily in tertiary Nashville submarkets will struggle, but amenity-rich, anchor-tenant-adjacent projects will outperform.

stories.title: Miami Freedom Park redevelopment, West Palm office tower among $370M-plus construction permits for week ending July 8, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest permit issued was a $193 million new construction permit for 1822 Northwest 37th Avenue in Miami, tied to the redevelopment of Miami Freedom Park near the Miami International Airport, which will include more than 1 million square feet of retail, dining, entertainment and office space, 750 hotel rooms, a 58-acre public park, a new City of Miami administration building and Inter Miami CF’s Nu Stadium — which is already complete.Job Filing Number: BD25026033 📊 New Building: The second-largest permit was a $180 million commercial permit for 850 South Quadrille Boulevard in West Palm Beach, where […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Freedom Park redevelopment permitted for $193M (1.2M sf retail, office, dining, entertainment, 750 hotel rooms, 58-acre park, Inter Miami stadium).
West Palm Point office tower (850 S. Quadrille, $180M permit) stalled for years, foreclosure lawsuit 2025; now moving.
South Florida permits week ending 7/8/26 topped $370M; Miami-area construction activity elevated despite prior office distress.
stories.summary: Miami Freedom Park, a massive mixed-use redevelopment near Miami International Airport, received a $193M construction permit for 1.2M sf of retail, office, dining, 750 hotel rooms, a 58-acre public park, and Inter Miami CF's new stadium (already complete). West Palm Point, a long-stalled $180M office tower, also received a permit after foreclosure litigation. South Florida issued over $370M in permits for the week ending 7/8/26.
stories.impact: Miami Freedom Park (Inter Miami stadium anchor) and broader South Florida mixed-use revival signals strong destination retail, hospitality, and sports-entertainment demand. For multifamily developers: stadium and large entertainment anchors drive incremental residential demand in adjacent/connected neighborhoods (Wynwood, Design District, Overtown); developers near Inter Miami's new facilities should prioritize transit-connected, walkable multifamily to capture stadium-goer and event-driven demand. However, office components (West Palm Point) remain challenged; avoid office-heavy mixed-use in secondary Florida markets.

stories.title: Naterra eyeing a $34M combination office and warehouse in Coppell
stories.excerpt: Naterra International, a cosmetics and beauty company, is looking to expand their operations in Coppell with a new $34 million combination headquarters and warehouse facility.  The facility is expected to span 442,010 square feet, with no additional information on how the space will be split between the office and the warehouse, according to the Dallas Business Journal.  Dallas-based Pross Design Group will be heading up the construction. The future headquarters of the company will sit at 1525 Point West Boulevard in the suburb just northwest of Dallas, and the facility should be completed around December 2027. The price of the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Naterra International (cosmetics, Baby Magic, Tree Hut, Splash brands) planning $34M, 442K sf HQ + warehouse in Coppell.
Facility at 1525 Point West Boulevard (~$77/sf); completion target December 2027; family-owned global manufacturer.
Naterra current HQ in Coppell; DFW region is thriving hub for beauty/personal care (Mary Kay, Sally Beauty, Jack Black, Cetaphil).
stories.summary: Naterra International, a family-owned cosmetics manufacturer (brands: Baby Magic, Tree Hut, Splash), is investing $34M in a new 442,010-sf headquarters and warehouse facility in Coppell, Texas. The facility at 1525 Point West Boulevard will combine office and warehouse; completion target is December 2027 at ~$77/sf. The company is consolidating from its current Coppell headquarters and reinforces DFW's strength as a beauty and personal-care supply hub.
stories.impact: Naterra expansion reflects ongoing corporate consolidation and growth in DFW's beauty/CPG sector, anchoring secondary-market employment in Coppell and surrounding Irving suburbs. For multifamily developers: office/warehouse combination facilities create diverse employment and attract skilled and operational workers; nearby multifamily should target workforce housing and can benefit from co-tenancy with corporate campuses. DFW's CPG/beauty cluster is a stable, long-term employment anchor supporting steady rent growth.

stories.title: San Antonio closes on three key Project Marvel parcels for $30M
stories.excerpt: San Antonio formally acquired federal properties downtown that are within the boundaries of a potential sports and entertainment district dubbed Project Marvel.  Officials for the General Services Administration and the city announced Monday that San Antonio paid $30 million for the federal office building at 727 East César Chávez Boulevard and two parking lots on Indianola Street. The purchase was funded by the NBA franchise the San Antonio Spurs, city manager Erik Walsh said in a statement. The San Antonio Business Journal reported that part of Project Marvel is set aside for the Spurs, who plan on building a new […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: San Antonio acquired three federal properties downtown from GSA for $30M, funded by San Antonio Spurs franchise.
Parcels include 727 E. César Chávez Blvd federal office building and two parking lots; part of Project Marvel ($1.2–1.5B).
Project Marvel: new Spurs arena, 50-acre mixed-use development, 1,000-room hotel, expanded Henry B. González Convention Center.
stories.summary: San Antonio, funded by the NBA's San Antonio Spurs, purchased three federal properties downtown for $30M from the GSA, including a federal office building and two parking lots. The parcels are core to Project Marvel, a $1.2–1.5B sports and entertainment district centered on a new Spurs arena. Full project also includes 50 acres of mixed-use, a 1,000-room hotel, and convention center expansion.
stories.impact: Project Marvel represents a major downtown anchoring event for San Antonio; acquisition of federal properties removes a key obstacle and unlocks long-stalled redevelopment. For multifamily developers: large sports/entertainment districts drive complementary residential demand (both residential conversion of existing structures and new construction near arena/entertainment nodes). Developers should scout land near Project Marvel boundaries for mixed-use or pure multifamily; arena district activation typically sustains 5–10 years of elevated rent growth in adjacent neighborhoods and generates lifestyle migration.
